proposition_text: On numerous occasions prior to go-live, Meridian warned TechFlow that the Platform was not ready and requested that go-live be deferred, and TechFlow ignored those warnings and proceeded to go-live on 18 November 2024.
summary: The proposition is comprehensively contradicted by the contemporaneous correspondence, the contractual record, and Meridian's own witness evidence, so it is strongly adverse. The factual reality is the reverse of what is pleaded: it was TechFlow (Marcus Reilly) who, on 24 October 2024, expressly recommended in writing that go-live be deferred to January 2025 to avoid the peak trading period, and it was Meridian's Programme Director, Helena Vance, who rejected that recommendation, instructed TechFlow to proceed on 18 November 2024, and stated she was content to take the risk on Meridian's side (doc_009). Helena Vance's own witness statement (doc_016) confirms she decided to go live despite TechFlow's advice and that 'going live just before Black Friday was optimistic'. The 18 November date itself arose from Meridian's own requested loyalty module change (doc_007, doc_010, doc_018), and Meridian signed a UAT acceptance certificate on 12 November 2024 recording no outstanding Severity 1/2 items (doc_008), undermining any contention that Meridian was warning the Platform was not ready. TechFlow's solicitors' letter (doc_015) makes the same point. No document anywhere records Meridian warning TechFlow the Platform was not ready or requesting deferral; the only deferral request in the bundle came from TechFlow. The proposition (drawn from paragraph 10 of the Particulars of Claim, doc_002) is therefore directly falsified by the surrounding evidence.

evidence.reasoning: The Master Services Agreement (14 March 2024) is relevant because it frames go-live and timetable. It provides that the supplier shall use reasonable endeavours to achieve the 'target go-live date' and that the implementation timetable 'is a planning estimate and may be adjusted through the change control procedure'. This undercuts the premise of the proposition that there was a firm readiness/deferral dispute driven by Meridian warnings, and shows the date was always adjustable by agreement (which is what later happened at Meridian's instance), rather than something Meridian was pressing to defer against an unwilling TechFlow.
evidence.excerpt: 3.1  The Supplier shall use reasonable endeavours to achieve the target go-live date stated in the SOW. The parties acknowledge that the implementation timetable is a planning estimate and may be adjusted through the change control procedure.

evidence.reasoning: The Statement of Work sets the original go-live target of 1 October 2024 and describes it as a target date subject to change control. It is contextually relevant to the timetable but says nothing about Meridian warning TechFlow of unreadiness or requesting deferral, so it neither supports nor materially contradicts the specific proposition about warnings; it is at most background context.
evidence.excerpt: 2.1  The go-live date of 1 October 2024 is a target date. The detailed implementation plan is a planning estimate and is subject to the change control procedure in clause 3 of the MSA.

evidence.reasoning: Change Order No. 3 (2 September 2024) is highly relevant and adverse. It records that, at Meridian's request, TechFlow would build a loyalty module not in the original scope, and that to accommodate this the go-live date was revised from 1 October to 18 November 2024, signed by Meridian's Head of Procurement. This shows the 18 November go-live date was the product of Meridian's own change request, not a date Meridian was warning against or seeking to defer. It is inconsistent with the proposition's picture of Meridian pressing TechFlow to defer go-live.
evidence.excerpt: 2.1  The parties agree that, to accommodate the additional works, the go-live date is revised from 1 October 2024 to 18 November 2024. The revised date applies to the Platform as a whole.

evidence.reasoning: The Phase 1 UAT Acceptance Certificate (12 November 2024), signed by Meridian's Priya Nair just six days before go-live, confirms Phase 1 passed UAT and is accepted, with no outstanding Severity 1 or 2 items. This is adverse to the proposition: shortly before go-live Meridian was formally accepting the Platform as having passed testing, not warning that it was not ready or requesting deferral.
evidence.excerpt: Meridian confirms that Phase 1 has passed User Acceptance Testing and is accepted, subject to the resolution of the outstanding minor (Severity 3 and 4) items listed in the snag log appended to the UAT report. No outstanding Severity 1 or Severity 2 items were recorded at the date of this certificate.

evidence.reasoning: This 24 October 2024 email thread is the single most directly contradictory document. It shows the opposite of the proposition: it was TechFlow's Marcus Reilly who recommended in writing that go-live be deferred to January 2025 because going live before Black Friday/Christmas carried real risk, and it was Meridian's Helena Vance who rejected that recommendation, instructed TechFlow to proceed on 18 November, and expressly said she was content to take that risk on Meridian's side. This squarely reverses the roles alleged in the proposition (Meridian warning/seeking deferral; TechFlow ignoring), making the document strongly adverse.
evidence.excerpt: Our clear recommendation is to defer go-live to mid-January 2025, after the peak trading period. Going live on 18 November, immediately before Black Friday and Christmas, carries real risk and removes our contingency. We can hold the 18 November date if Meridian instructs us to, but we would be doing so against our advice.

evidence.reasoning: This 21-27 August 2024 email thread shows Meridian (Helena Vance) requesting the loyalty module and accepting there may be a knock-on to the go-live date, with TechFlow responding that go-live would move to 18 November 2024. It is adverse to the proposition because it establishes that Meridian itself drove the change that produced the 18 November date and was willing to discuss a revised date, which is inconsistent with Meridian later being characterised as warning TechFlow not to proceed and seeking deferral.
evidence.excerpt: We accept there may be a knock-on to the go-live date and we are willing to discuss a revised date to accommodate it.

evidence.reasoning: TechFlow's solicitors' response (7 February 2025) directly rebuts the proposition, recording that TechFlow expressly recommended deferral in writing on 24 October 2024 and that Meridian's Programme Director rejected that recommendation and instructed TechFlow to proceed, accepting the risk. This contemporaneous statement of TechFlow's case corroborates doc_009 and is adverse to the proposition's account that Meridian warned and TechFlow ignored.
evidence.excerpt: Our client expressly recommended, in writing on 24 October 2024, that go-live be deferred to January 2025 to avoid the peak trading period. Your client’s Programme Director rejected that recommendation and instructed our client to proceed on 18 November 2024, accepting the associated risk.

evidence.reasoning: Helena Vance is Meridian's own Programme Director and the very person who would have given any warnings. Her witness statement confirms that, as go-live approached, TechFlow's Marcus Reilly recommended in writing delaying to January 2025, and that she decided Meridian should go live on 18 November despite TechFlow's advice, conceding that doing so just before Black Friday 'was optimistic'. This is decisive admission evidence from Meridian's own side that contradicts the proposition: the deferral request came from TechFlow, and Meridian (not TechFlow) made the decision to proceed.
evidence.excerpt: As we approached go-live, Marcus Reilly recommended in writing that we delay until January 2025, after the Christmas period. I decided that we should go live on 18 November 2024. ... I took the decision to proceed despite TechFlow’s advice. With hindsight, going live just before Black Friday was optimistic.

evidence.reasoning: Priya Nair (Meridian's Head of Procurement) confirms she signed Change Order No. 3 which added the Meridian-requested loyalty module and revised go-live to 18 November 2024, and that she signed the Phase 1 Acceptance Certificate on 12 November 2024 with no critical items outstanding. This corroborates that the 18 November date stemmed from Meridian's own change and that Meridian was accepting the Platform shortly before go-live, which is inconsistent with the proposition that Meridian was repeatedly warning the Platform was not ready and seeking deferral.
evidence.excerpt: On 2 September 2024 I signed Change Order No. 3. This added the customer loyalty module, which Helena Vance had asked TechFlow to provide ... and it revised the go-live date from 1 October to 18 November 2024. ... On 12 November 2024 I signed the Phase 1 Acceptance Certificate, confirming that Phase 1 of the platform had passed user acceptance testing. There were some minor snags listed, but no critical items were outstanding at that point.

evidence.reasoning: This is the source pleading from which the proposition is drawn (paragraph 10 of the Particulars of Claim). On its face it asserts the proposition verbatim, so as a pleaded allegation it supports the proposition. However, it is a self-serving statement of case rather than independent evidence, and it is contradicted by the contemporaneous documents and Meridian's own witnesses; it is retained here as the origin of the allegation.
evidence.excerpt: 10.On numerous occasions prior to go-live, Meridian warned TechFlow that the Platform was not ready and requested that go-live be deferred. TechFlow ignored those warnings and proceeded to go-live on 18 November 2024.

evidence.reasoning: The Claim Form gives only brief details of a breach/negligence/misrepresentation claim and the value; it does not address whether Meridian warned TechFlow of unreadiness or requested deferral of go-live.
The Phase 1 Order Form sets out items and charges and payment terms; it says nothing about go-live readiness warnings or any request to defer go-live.

The Deed of Variation No. 1 varies only the payment milestone profile and expressly does not vary scope or the target go-live date; it does not address any Meridian warning or deferral request regarding go-live readiness.

This email concerns the root cause of the 25 November 2024 outage (a Northgate Telecom network failure, post go-live). It does not address pre-go-live warnings about readiness or any request to defer go-live.

This internal Meridian email concerns causes of poor December trading (DC flood, weak market, stock-sync issues) after go-live. It does not address whether Meridian warned TechFlow the Platform was not ready or requested deferral before go-live.

The defect log records defects raised from 19 November 2024 onward (post go-live). It is relevant to defect/quality issues but does not address pre-go-live warnings by Meridian or any deferral request, so it does not bear on this proposition.

The Notice of Termination letter asserts late delivery, post go-live failures and unfitness for purpose, but does not address whether Meridian warned TechFlow of unreadiness before go-live or requested deferral; it is silent on the specific factual matter in the proposition.

Raymond Okafor's witness statement addresses post go-live operational difficulties and the various non-Platform causes of poor December trading. It does not address any pre-go-live warning by Meridian or any request to defer go-live.

Dr Whitfield's IT expert report addresses Platform availability and defects after go-live; it does not address whether Meridian warned TechFlow that the Platform was not ready or requested deferral before go-live.
Ms Greenhalgh's quantum expert report addresses wasted expenditure and loss of profit; it does not address the pre-go-live warnings or deferral request in the proposition.
